title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read an article about cycling and the transformation of urban transport. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Two-Wheeled City
content.subtitle: An article about cycling, urban planning, and the future of city transport.
content.text:
For most of the twentieth century, the car was the undisputed organising principle of the Western city. Roads were widened, neighbourhoods demolished, and urban layouts redesigned to accommodate the vehicle that symbolised modernity and personal freedom. Today, in a growing number of cities, that logic is being challenged — and in some places, visibly reversed. [31] The bicycle, once dismissed as an anachronism or a recreational accessory, has re-emerged as a serious contender for the urban commute, backed by investment in infrastructure, changes in public attitude, and the environmental pressures that make rethinking car dependency urgent. [/31]

The evidence from cities that have invested seriously in cycling is broadly encouraging. In Copenhagen and Amsterdam, cycling accounts for a substantial proportion of all journeys — not because residents are unusually athletic or environmentally virtuous, but because the infrastructure makes cycling the most convenient choice. [32] The lesson these cities offer is not that cycling is natural or inevitable, but that it responds predictably to conditions: make it safe, make it direct, and make it connected to other forms of transport, and people will choose it in large numbers. [/32]

Opponents of urban cycling infrastructure tend to make two arguments: that it is impractical in cities without established cycling cultures, and that reducing road space for motor vehicles creates congestion that harms businesses and emergency services. [33] Both concerns are worth taking seriously, but the evidence is mixed. Studies in cities that have reduced car lanes to create protected cycle paths have often found that predicted congestion fails to materialise — a phenomenon sometimes referred to as 'traffic evaporation', as some drivers shift to other modes once road capacity decreases. [/33]

The class politics of urban cycling are more complicated than the issue's association with progressive urban professionals might suggest. In many cities, cycling is most common among lower-income residents who cannot afford other transport options. [34] Yet investment in cycling infrastructure — particularly in gentrifying neighbourhoods — has sometimes been accompanied by rising property values, the displacement of working-class residents, and a reimagining of public space that benefits wealthier newcomers more than established communities. [/34] Advocates of cycling infrastructure must grapple with these dynamics rather than assuming that good transport policy is automatically socially equitable.

Electric bikes have added a new dimension to this picture. By significantly extending the range and gradient that riders can manage comfortably, e-bikes have expanded cycling's demographic reach — making it accessible to older riders, those with health conditions, and commuters who arrive at work unwilling to shower and change. [35] Their rapid adoption has caught many city planners off guard, as infrastructure designed for conventional bicycles may not adequately account for the higher speeds and greater weights that e-bikes typically involve. [/35]

The city of the future is unlikely to be car-free, but the balance is shifting. [36] What is becoming clear is that the post-war model of the car-centric city — which sacrificed so much public space, community cohesion, and air quality at the altar of vehicular convenience — is no longer the only imaginable template for urban life. [/36]

questions.question: The author describes the bicycle's current position in urban transport as
options: A) a niche option appealing mainly to young professionals.
B) a serious alternative to the car, supported by infrastructure and public pressure.
C) still primarily a recreational activity in most cities.
D) a fashion that is unlikely to survive when road space becomes limited.
questions.explanation: The bicycle is described as 'a serious contender for the urban commute, backed by investment in infrastructure, changes in public attitude, and environmental pressures'.

questions.question: What conclusion does the author draw from the examples of Copenhagen and Amsterdam?
options: A) Cycling naturally thrives in cities with flat geography.
B) Residents of Northern European cities have a stronger environmental commitment.
C) High cycling rates are the result of good infrastructure, not cultural traits.
D) Cycling is most successful when combined with cycling education programmes.
questions.explanation: The author states these cities have high cycling rates 'not because residents are unusually athletic or environmentally virtuous, but because the infrastructure makes cycling the most convenient choice'.

questions.question: The concept of 'traffic evaporation' refers to the finding that
options: A) air pollution decreases when fewer cars travel through city centres.
B) reducing road space does not always cause the expected increase in traffic.
C) traffic naturally moves away from city centres as populations grow.
D) congestion is highest during hot weather when more people choose to drive.
questions.explanation: 'Traffic evaporation' describes cases where 'predicted congestion fails to materialise' after car lanes are reduced, as some drivers switch to other transport modes.

questions.question: The author raises the issue of gentrification to suggest that
options: A) cycling infrastructure is only built in wealthy parts of cities.
B) working-class residents oppose cycling infrastructure in their neighbourhoods.
C) investment in cycling does not automatically benefit all residents equally.
D) property developers deliberately use cycling paths to increase land values.
questions.explanation: The author says cycling infrastructure 'sometimes' benefits wealthier newcomers more than established communities, arguing advocates must 'grapple with these dynamics' rather than assuming equity.

questions.question: The arrival of e-bikes has created difficulties for city planners because
options: A) they are too expensive for most commuters to afford.
B) existing cycling infrastructure may not be designed to handle them safely.
C) they are frequently stolen, discouraging investment in cycle lanes.
D) their motors make them unsuitable for use in city centres.
questions.explanation: 'Infrastructure designed for conventional bicycles may not adequately account for the higher speeds and greater weights that e-bikes typically involve.'

questions.question: In the final paragraph, the author suggests that
options: A) all private cars will eventually be banned from major cities.
B) urban cycling has now definitively replaced the car as the primary transport mode.
C) the dominance of car-centric urban design is starting to be questioned.
D) city planners have found the ideal balance between cycling and driving.
questions.explanation: The author says the car-centric city model 'is no longer the only imaginable template', implying it is being questioned rather than having been replaced or fully resolved.
